questionText: State the range of the function $y = \sec x$, and explain why no values of $y$ lie strictly between $-1$ and $1$.

steps.explanation: Secant is one divided by cosine, so its possible outputs are controlled entirely by the possible values of $\cos x$.

steps.description: State the values cosine can take
steps.explanation: Cosine only ever takes values between $-1$ and $1$. We must also exclude $\cos x = 0$, since dividing by zero is undefined (those points give the asymptotes).
When cosine is positive but at most $1$, its reciprocal is at least $1$: dividing $1$ by a number no bigger than $1$ can never give an answer below $1$. So all positive secant values are $\ge 1$.
Similarly, when cosine is negative but no smaller than $-1$, its reciprocal is at most $-1$. So all negative secant values are $\le -1$.
Because $|\cos x|$ never exceeds $1$, its reciprocal is never smaller than $1$ in size. That is exactly why secant can never land strictly between $-1$ and $1$ — there is a gap around $0$.
Putting the two pieces together, every secant value is either at most $-1$ or at least $1$.
This describes all outputs of $y=\sec x$: everything from $1$ upward together with everything from $-1$ downward.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The range of $y=\sec x$ is $y \le -1$ or $y \ge 1$; no output lies strictly between $-1$ and $1$ because $|\sec x| = \dfrac{1}{|\cos x|} \ge 1$.
